claim: The study performed walk-forward validation with a 4-year training window, a 2-year validation window, a 1-year step size, and a 30-day embargo, generating nine sequential train/validation splits per asset covering validation periods 2014–2024.

The authors found overwhelming evidence of non-identifiability: multiple circuits perfectly replicated model behavior, multiple interpretations existed for a given circuit, several algorithms were perfectly causally aligned with the neural computation, and a single algorithm was causally aligned with different subspaces of the network.

The authors fine-tune the VIRAL model using a Mixture-of-Experts LoRA (MoE-LoRA) adapter with four experts (rank 64, top-2 routing) applied to the feed-forward network output projection, and standard LoRA (rank 64) applied to the attention query, key, and value layers.
sources: No indexed academic or reliable publicly searchable source found describing exactly this VIRAL + MoE-LoRA configuration

claim: The authors conducted an anonymous survey of the 26 students enrolled in the course (19 responded) and found that 32 % of respondents reported the course had a "good extent" of influence on their academic or career plans, while another 32 % reported influence "to a great extent".
sources: No indexed academic or institutional survey matching these specific numbers was found during web search

claim: The authors demonstrate that increasing the number of servers from two to four leads to a monotonic increase in client-selection conflict count for all evaluated algorithms, but RL-CRP consistently yields fewer conflicts than ENSAC and FedAvg.
sources: No indexed academic or reputable publication found that directly reports this federated learning comparison
